sourceRefs.fallbackText: The pattern matching process takes as input a pattern (following case ) and a subject value (following match ). Phrases to describe the process include “the pattern is matched with (or against) the subject value” and “we match the pattern against (or with) the subject value”.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The pattern matching process takes as input a pattern (following case ) and a subject value (following match ). Phrases to describe the process include “the pattern is matched with (or against) the subject value” and “we

highSignalBlocks.text: The pattern matching process takes as input a pattern (following case ) and a subject value (following match ). Phrases to describe the process include “the pattern is matched with (or against) the subject value” and “we match the pattern against (or with) the subject value”.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The primary outcome of pattern matching is success or failure. In case of success we may say “the pattern succeeds”, “the match succeeds”, or “the pattern matches the subject value”.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The primary outcome of pattern matching is success or failure. In case of success we may say “the pattern succeeds”, “the match succeeds”, or “the pattern matches the subject value”.

highSignalBlocks.text: The primary outcome of pattern matching is success or failure. In case of success we may say “the pattern succeeds”, “the match succeeds”, or “the pattern matches the subject value”.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In many cases a pattern contains subpatterns, and success or failure is determined by the success or failure of matching those subpatterns against the value (e.g., for OR patterns) or against parts of the value (e.g., for sequence patterns). This process typically processes the subpatterns from left to right until the overall outcome is determined. E.g., an
sourceRefs.textQuote: In many cases a pattern contains subpatterns, and success or failure is determined by the success or failure of matching those subpatterns against the value (e.g., for OR patterns) or against parts of the value (e.g., fo

highSignalBlocks.text: In many cases a pattern contains subpatterns, and success or failure is determined by the success or failure of matching those subpatterns against the value (e.g., for OR patterns) or against parts of the value (e.g., for sequence patterns). This process typically processes the subpatterns from left to right until the overall outcome is determined. E.g., an OR pattern succeeds at the first succeeding subpattern, while a sequence patterns fails at the first failing subpattern.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A secondary outcome of pattern matching may be one or more name bindings. We may say “the pattern binds a value to a name”. When subpatterns tried until the first success, only the bindings due to the successful subpattern are valid; when trying until the first failure, the bindings are merged. Several more rules, explained below, apply to these cases.
sourceRefs.textQuote: A secondary outcome of pattern matching may be one or more name bindings. We may say “the pattern binds a value to a name”. When subpatterns tried until the first success, only the bindings due to the successful subpatte

highSignalBlocks.text: A secondary outcome of pattern matching may be one or more name bindings. We may say “the pattern binds a value to a name”. When subpatterns tried until the first success, only the bindings due to the successful subpattern are valid; when trying until the first failure, the bindings are merged. Several more rules, explained below, apply to these cases.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The match and case keywords are soft keywords, i.e. they are not reserved words in other grammatical contexts (including at the start of a line if there is no colon where expected). This implies that they are recognized as keywords when part of a match statement or case block only, and are allowed to be used in all other contexts as variable or argument name
sourceRefs.textQuote: The match and case keywords are soft keywords, i.e. they are not reserved words in other grammatical contexts (including at the start of a line if there is no colon where expected). This implies that they are recognized

highSignalBlocks.text: The match and case keywords are soft keywords, i.e. they are not reserved words in other grammatical contexts (including at the start of a line if there is no colon where expected). This implies that they are recognized as keywords when part of a match statement or case block only, and are allowed to be used in all other contexts as variable or argument names.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The match statement first evaluates the subject expression. If a comma is present a tuple is constructed using the standard rules.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The match statement first evaluates the subject expression. If a comma is present a tuple is constructed using the standard rules.

highSignalBlocks.text: The match statement first evaluates the subject expression. If a comma is present a tuple is constructed using the standard rules.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The resulting subject value is then used to select the first case block whose patterns succeeds matching it and whose guard condition (if present) is “truthy”. If no case blocks qualify the match statement is complete; otherwise, the block of the selected case block is executed. The usual rules for executing a block nested inside a compound statement apply (
sourceRefs.textQuote: The resulting subject value is then used to select the first case block whose patterns succeeds matching it and whose guard condition (if present) is “truthy”. If no case blocks qualify the match statement is complete; o

highSignalBlocks.text: The resulting subject value is then used to select the first case block whose patterns succeeds matching it and whose guard condition (if present) is “truthy”. If no case blocks qualify the match statement is complete; otherwise, the block of the selected case block is executed. The usual rules for executing a block nested inside a compound statement apply (e.g. an if statement).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Name bindings made during a successful pattern match outlive the executed block and can be used after the match statement.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Name bindings made during a successful pattern match outlive the executed block and can be used after the match statement.

highSignalBlocks.text: Name bindings made during a successful pattern match outlive the executed block and can be used after the match statement.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: During failed pattern matches, some subpatterns may succeed. For example, while matching the pattern (0, x, 1) with the value [0, 1, 2] , the subpattern x may succeed if the list elements are matched from left to right. The implementation may choose to either make persistent bindings for those partial matches or not. User code including a match statement sho
sourceRefs.textQuote: During failed pattern matches, some subpatterns may succeed. For example, while matching the pattern (0, x, 1) with the value [0, 1, 2] , the subpattern x may succeed if the list elements are matched from left to right.

highSignalBlocks.text: During failed pattern matches, some subpatterns may succeed. For example, while matching the pattern (0, x, 1) with the value [0, 1, 2] , the subpattern x may succeed if the list elements are matched from left to right. The implementation may choose to either make persistent bindings for those partial matches or not. User code including a match statement should not rely on the bindings being made for a failed match, but also shouldn’t assume that variables are unchanged by a failed match. This part of the behavior is left intentionally unspecified so different implementations can add optimizations, and to prevent introducing semantic restrictions that could limit the extensibility of this feature.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The precise pattern binding rules vary per pattern type and are specified below.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The precise pattern binding rules vary per pattern type and are specified below.

highSignalBlocks.text: The precise pattern binding rules vary per pattern type and are specified below.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If a guard is present on a case block, once the pattern or patterns in the case block succeed, the expression in the guard is evaluated. If this raises an exception, the exception bubbles up. Otherwise, if the condition is “truthy” the case block is selected; if it is “falsy” the case block is not selected.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If a guard is present on a case block, once the pattern or patterns in the case block succeed, the expression in the guard is evaluated. If this raises an exception, the exception bubbles up. Otherwise, if the condition

highSignalBlocks.text: If a guard is present on a case block, once the pattern or patterns in the case block succeed, the expression in the guard is evaluated. If this raises an exception, the exception bubbles up. Otherwise, if the condition is “truthy” the case block is selected; if it is “falsy” the case block is not selected.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Since guards are expressions they are allowed to have side effects. Guard evaluation must proceed from the first to the last case block, one at a time, skipping case blocks whose pattern(s) don’t all succeed. (I.e., even if determining whether those patterns succeed may happen out of order, guard evaluation must happen in order.) Guard evaluation must stop o
sourceRefs.textQuote: Since guards are expressions they are allowed to have side effects. Guard evaluation must proceed from the first to the last case block, one at a time, skipping case blocks whose pattern(s) don’t all succeed. (I.e., even

highSignalBlocks.text: Since guards are expressions they are allowed to have side effects. Guard evaluation must proceed from the first to the last case block, one at a time, skipping case blocks whose pattern(s) don’t all succeed. (I.e., even if determining whether those patterns succeed may happen out of order, guard evaluation must happen in order.) Guard evaluation must stop once a case block is selected.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A pattern is considered irrefutable if we can prove from its syntax alone that it will always succeed. In particular, capture patterns and wildcard patterns are irrefutable, and so are AS patterns whose left-hand side is irrefutable, OR patterns containing at least one irrefutable pattern, and parenthesized irrefutable patterns.
sourceRefs.textQuote: A pattern is considered irrefutable if we can prove from its syntax alone that it will always succeed. In particular, capture patterns and wildcard patterns are irrefutable, and so are AS patterns whose left-hand side is

highSignalBlocks.text: A pattern is considered irrefutable if we can prove from its syntax alone that it will always succeed. In particular, capture patterns and wildcard patterns are irrefutable, and so are AS patterns whose left-hand side is irrefutable, OR patterns containing at least one irrefutable pattern, and parenthesized irrefutable patterns.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A case block is considered irrefutable if it has no guard and its pattern is irrefutable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: A case block is considered irrefutable if it has no guard and its pattern is irrefutable.

highSignalBlocks.text: A case block is considered irrefutable if it has no guard and its pattern is irrefutable.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A match statement may have at most one irrefutable case block, and it must be last.
sourceRefs.textQuote: A match statement may have at most one irrefutable case block, and it must be last.

highSignalBlocks.text: A match statement may have at most one irrefutable case block, and it must be last.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An AS pattern matches the OR pattern on the left of the as keyword against the subject. If this fails, the AS pattern fails. Otherwise, the AS pattern binds the subject to the name on the right of the as keyword and succeeds.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When two or more patterns are separated by vertical bars ( | ), this is called an OR pattern. (A single closed pattern is just that.)
sourceRefs.textQuote: When two or more patterns are separated by vertical bars ( | ), this is called an OR pattern. (A single closed pattern is just that.)

highSignalBlocks.text: When two or more patterns are separated by vertical bars ( | ), this is called an OR pattern. (A single closed pattern is just that.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Only the final subpattern may be irrefutable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Only the final subpattern may be irrefutable.

highSignalBlocks.text: Only the final subpattern may be irrefutable.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Each subpattern must bind the same set of names.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Each subpattern must bind the same set of names.

highSignalBlocks.text: Each subpattern must bind the same set of names.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An OR pattern matches each of its subpatterns in turn to the subject, until one succeeds. The OR pattern is then deemed to succeed. If none of the subpatterns succeed the OR pattern fails.
sourceRefs.textQuote: An OR pattern matches each of its subpatterns in turn to the subject, until one succeeds. The OR pattern is then deemed to succeed. If none of the subpatterns succeed the OR pattern fails.

highSignalBlocks.text: An OR pattern matches each of its subpatterns in turn to the subject, until one succeeds. The OR pattern is then deemed to succeed. If none of the subpatterns succeed the OR pattern fails.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Triple-quoted strings are supported. Raw strings and byte strings are supported. F-strings are not supported.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The single underscore ( _ ) is not a capture pattern (this is what !"_" expresses). It is treated as a wildcard pattern .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A capture pattern always succeeds. It binds the subject value to the name using the scoping rules for name binding established for the walrus operator in PEP 572 . (Summary: the name becomes a local variable in the closest containing function scope unless there’s an applicable nonlocal or global statement.)

(Summary: the name becomes a local variable in the closest containing function scope unless there’s an applicable nonlocal or global statement.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In a given pattern, a given name may be bound only once. This disallows for example case x, x: ... but allows case [x] | x: ... .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A fixed-length sequence pattern matches the subpatterns to corresponding items of the subject sequence, from left to right. If this succeeds, the star subpattern matches a list formed of the remaining subject items, with items removed from the end corresponding to the non-star subpatterns following the star subpattern. The remaining non-star subpatterns are then matched to the corresponding subject items, as for a fixed-length sequence.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The only side-effect produced explicitly by the matching process is the binding of names. However, the process relies on attribute access, instance checks, len() , equality and item access on the subject and some of its components. It also evaluates value patterns and the class name of class patterns. While none of those typically create any side-effects, in
sourceRefs.textQuote: The only side-effect produced explicitly by the matching process is the binding of names. However, the process relies on attribute access, instance checks, len() , equality and item access on the subject and some of its

highSignalBlocks.text: The only side-effect produced explicitly by the matching process is the binding of names. However, the process relies on attribute access, instance checks, len() , equality and item access on the subject and some of its components. It also evaluates value patterns and the class name of class patterns. While none of those typically create any side-effects, in theory they could. This proposal intentionally leaves out any specification of what methods are called or how many times. This behavior is therefore undefined and user code should not rely on it.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Another undefined behavior is the binding of variables by capture patterns that are followed (in the same case block) by another pattern that fails. These may happen earlier or later depending on the implementation strategy, the only constraint being that capture variables must be set before guards that use them explicitly are evaluated. If a guard consists
sourceRefs.textQuote: Another undefined behavior is the binding of variables by capture patterns that are followed (in the same case block) by another pattern that fails. These may happen earlier or later depending on the implementation strat

highSignalBlocks.text: Another undefined behavior is the binding of variables by capture patterns that are followed (in the same case block) by another pattern that fails. These may happen earlier or later depending on the implementation strategy, the only constraint being that capture variables must be set before guards that use them explicitly are evaluated. If a guard consists of an and clause, evaluation of the operands may even be interspersed with pattern matching, as long as left-to-right evaluation order is maintained.
